text: Enter Chorus.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Two households, both alike in dignity, In fair Verona, where we lay our scene, From ancient grudge break to new mutiny, Where civil blood makes civil hands unclean. From forth the fatal loins of these two foes A pair of star-cross'd lovers take their life; Whose misadventur'd piteous overthrows Doth with their death bury their parents' strife. The fearful passage of their death-mark'd love, And the continuance of their parents' rage, Which, but their children's end, naught could remove, Is now the two hours' traffic of our stage; The which if you with patient ears attend, What here shall miss, our toil shall strive to mend.
speaker: CHORUS

text: Exit.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter Sampson and Gregory (with swords and bucklers) of the house
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: of Capulet.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Gregory, on my word, we'll not carry coals.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: No, for then we should be colliers.
speaker: GREGORY

text: I mean, an we be in choler, we'll draw.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: Ay, while you live, draw your neck out of collar.
speaker: GREGORY

text: I strike quickly, being moved.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: But thou art not quickly moved to strike.
speaker: GREGORY

text: A dog of the house of Montague moves me.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: To move is to stir, and to be valiant is to stand. Therefore, if thou art moved, thou runn'st away.
speaker: GREGORY

text: A dog of that house shall move me to stand. I will take the wall of any man or maid of Montague's.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: That shows thee a weak slave; for the weakest goes to the wall.
speaker: GREGORY

text: 'Tis true; and therefore women, being the weaker vessels, are ever thrust to the wall. Therefore I will push Montague's men from the wall and thrust his maids to the wall.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: The quarrel is between our masters and us their men.
speaker: GREGORY

text: 'Tis all one. I will show myself a tyrant. When I have fought with the men, I will be cruel with the maids- I will cut off their heads.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: The heads of the maids?
speaker: GREGORY

text: Ay, the heads of the maids, or their maidenheads. Take it in what sense thou wilt.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: They must take it in sense that feel it.
speaker: GREGORY

text: Me they shall feel while I am able to stand; and 'tis known I am a pretty piece of flesh.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: 'Tis well thou art not fish; if thou hadst, thou hadst been poor-John. Draw thy tool! Here comes two of the house of Montagues.
speaker: GREGORY

text: Enter two other Servingmen [Abram and Balthasar].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: My naked weapon is out. Quarrel! I will back thee.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: How? turn thy back and run?
speaker: GREGORY

text: Fear me not.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: No, marry. I fear thee!
speaker: GREGORY

text: Let us take the law of our sides; let them begin.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: I will frown as I pass by, and let them take it as they list.
speaker: GREGORY

text: Nay, as they dare. I will bite my thumb at them; which is disgrace to them, if they bear it.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?
speaker: ABRAM

text: I do bite my thumb, sir.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?
speaker: ABRAM

text: [aside to Gregory] Is the law of our side if I say ay?
speaker: SAMPSON

text: [aside to Sampson] No.
speaker: GREGORY

text: No, sir, I do not bite my thumb at you, sir; but I bite my thumb, sir.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: Do you quarrel, sir?
speaker: GREGORY

text: Quarrel, sir? No, sir.
speaker: ABRAM

text: But if you do, sir, am for you. I serve as good a man as you.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: No better.
speaker: ABRAM

text: Well, sir.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: Enter Benvolio.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: [aside to Sampson] Say 'better.' Here comes one of my master's kinsmen.
speaker: GREGORY

text: Yes, better, sir.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: You lie.
speaker: ABRAM

text: Draw, if you be men. Gregory, remember thy swashing blow. They fight.
speaker: SAMPSON

text: Part, fools! [Beats down their swords.] Put up your swords. You know not what you do.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Enter Tybalt.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: What, art thou drawn among these heartless hinds? Turn thee Benvolio! look upon thy death.
speaker: TYBALT

text: I do but keep the peace. Put up thy sword, Or manage it to part these men with me.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: What, drawn, and talk of peace? I hate the word As I hate hell, all Montagues, and thee. Have at thee, coward!                            They fight.
speaker: TYBALT

text: Enter an officer, and three or four Citizens with clubs or
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: partisans.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Clubs, bills, and partisans! Strike! beat them down!
speaker: OFFICER

text: Down with the Capulets! Down with the Montagues!
speaker: CITIZENS

text: Enter Old Capulet in his gown, and his Wife.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: What noise is this? Give me my long sword, ho!
speaker: CAPULET

text: A crutch, a crutch! Why call you for a sword?
speaker: WIFE

text: My sword, I say! Old Montague is come And flourishes his blade in spite of me.
speaker: CAPULET

text: Enter Old Montague and his Wife.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Thou villain Capulet!- Hold me not, let me go.
speaker: MONTAGUE

text: Wife. Thou shalt not stir one foot to seek a foe.
speaker: M

text: Enter Prince Escalus, with his Train.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Rebellious subjects, enemies to peace, Profaners of this neighbour-stained steel- Will they not hear? What, ho! you men, you beasts, That quench the fire of your pernicious rage With purple fountains issuing from your veins! On pain of torture, from those bloody hands Throw your mistempered weapons to the ground And hear the sentence of your moved prince. Three civil brawls, bred of an airy word By thee, old Capulet, and Montague, Have thrice disturb'd the quiet of our streets And made Verona's ancient citizens Cast by their grave beseeming ornaments To wield old partisans, in hands as old, Cank'red with peace, to part your cank'red hate. If ever you disturb our streets again, Your lives shall pay the forfeit of the peace. For this time all the rest depart away. You, Capulet, shall go along with me; And, Montague, come you this afternoon, To know our farther pleasure in this case, To old Freetown, our common judgment place. Once more, on pain of death, all men depart.
speaker: PRINCE

text: Exeunt [all but Montague, his Wife, and Benvolio].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Who set this ancient quarrel new abroach? Speak, nephew, were you by when it began?
speaker: MONTAGUE

text: Here were the servants of your adversary And yours, close fighting ere I did approach. I drew to part them. In the instant came The fiery Tybalt, with his sword prepar'd; Which, as he breath'd defiance to my ears, He swung about his head and cut the winds, Who, nothing hurt withal, hiss'd him in scorn. While we were interchanging thrusts and blows, Came more and more, and fought on part and part, Till the Prince came, who parted either part.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Wife. O, where is Romeo? Saw you him to-day? Right glad I am he was not at this fray.
speaker: M

text: Madam, an hour before the worshipp'd sun Peer'd forth the golden window of the East, A troubled mind drave me to walk abroad; Where, underneath the grove of sycamore That westward rooteth from the city's side, So early walking did I see your son. Towards him I made; but he was ware of me And stole into the covert of the wood. I- measuring his affections by my own, Which then most sought where most might not be found, Being one too many by my weary self- Pursu'd my humour, not Pursuing his, And gladly shunn'd who gladly fled from me.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Many a morning hath he there been seen, With tears augmenting the fresh morning's dew, Adding to clouds more clouds with his deep sighs; But all so soon as the all-cheering sun Should in the farthest East bean to draw The shady curtains from Aurora's bed, Away from light steals home my heavy son And private in his chamber pens himself, Shuts up his windows, locks fair daylight out And makes himself an artificial night. Black and portentous must this humour prove Unless good counsel may the cause remove.
speaker: MONTAGUE

text: My noble uncle, do you know the cause?
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: I neither know it nor can learn of him.
speaker: MONTAGUE

text: Have you importun'd him by any means?
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Both by myself and many other friends; But he, his own affections' counsellor, Is to himself- I will not say how true- But to himself so secret and so close, So far from sounding and discovery, As is the bud bit with an envious worm Ere he can spread his sweet leaves to the air Or dedicate his beauty to the sun. Could we but learn from whence his sorrows grow, We would as willingly give cure as know.
speaker: MONTAGUE

text: Enter Romeo.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: See, where he comes. So please you step aside, I'll know his grievance, or be much denied.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: I would thou wert so happy by thy stay To hear true shrift. Come, madam, let's away,
speaker: MONTAGUE

text: Exeunt [Montague and Wife].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Good morrow, cousin.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Is the day so young?
speaker: ROMEO

text: But new struck nine.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Ah me! sad hours seem long. Was that my father that went hence so fast?
speaker: ROMEO

text: It was. What sadness lengthens Romeo's hours?
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Not having that which having makes them short.
speaker: ROMEO

text: In love?
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Out-
speaker: ROMEO

text: Of love?
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Out of her favour where I am in love.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Alas that love, so gentle in his view, Should be so tyrannous and rough in proof!
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Alas that love, whose view is muffled still, Should without eyes see pathways to his will! Where shall we dine? O me! What fray was here? Yet tell me not, for I have heard it all. Here's much to do with hate, but more with love. Why then, O brawling love! O loving hate! O anything, of nothing first create! O heavy lightness! serious vanity! Misshapen chaos of well-seeming forms! Feather of lead, bright smoke, cold fire, sick health! Still-waking sleep, that is not what it is This love feel I, that feel no love in this. Dost thou not laugh?
speaker: ROMEO

text: No, coz, I rather weep.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Good heart, at what?
speaker: ROMEO

text: At thy good heart's oppression.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Why, such is love's transgression. Griefs of mine own lie heavy in my breast, Which thou wilt propagate, to have it prest With more of thine. This love that thou hast shown Doth add more grief to too much of mine own. Love is a smoke rais'd with the fume of sighs; Being purg'd, a fire sparkling in lovers' eyes; Being vex'd, a sea nourish'd with lovers' tears. What is it else? A madness most discreet, A choking gall, and a preserving sweet. Farewell, my coz.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Soft! I will go along. An if you leave me so, you do me wrong.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Tut! I have lost myself; I am not here: This is not Romeo, he's some other where.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Tell me in sadness, who is that you love?
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: What, shall I groan and tell thee?
speaker: ROMEO

text: Groan? Why, no; But sadly tell me who.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Bid a sick man in sadness make his will. Ah, word ill urg'd to one that is so ill! In sadness, cousin, I do love a woman.
speaker: ROMEO

text: I aim'd so near when I suppos'd you lov'd.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: A right good markman! And she's fair I love.
speaker: ROMEO

text: A right fair mark, fair coz, is soonest hit.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Well, in that hit you miss. She'll not be hit With Cupid's arrow. She hath Dian's wit, And, in strong proof of chastity well arm'd, From Love's weak childish bow she lives unharm'd. She will not stay the siege of loving terms, Nor bide th' encounter of assailing eyes, Nor ope her lap to saint-seducing gold. O, she's rich in beauty; only poor That, when she dies, with beauty dies her store.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Then she hath sworn that she will still live chaste?
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: She hath, and in that sparing makes huge waste; For beauty, starv'd with her severity, Cuts beauty off from all posterity. She is too fair, too wise, wisely too fair, To merit bliss by making me despair. She hath forsworn to love, and in that vow Do I live dead that live to tell it now.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Be rul'd by me: forget to think of her.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: O, teach me how I should forget to think!
speaker: ROMEO

text: By giving liberty unto thine eyes. Examine other beauties.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: 'Tis the way To call hers (exquisite) in question more. These happy masks that kiss fair ladies' brows, Being black puts us in mind they hide the fair. He that is strucken blind cannot forget The precious treasure of his eyesight lost. Show me a mistress that is passing fair, What doth her beauty serve but as a note Where I may read who pass'd that passing fair?
speaker: ROMEO

text: Thou canst not teach me to forget.
speaker: FAREWELL

text: I'll pay that doctrine, or else die in debt.      Exeunt.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Enter Capulet, County Paris, and [Servant] -the Clown.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: But Montague is bound as well as I, In penalty alike; and 'tis not hard, I think, For men so old as we to keep the peace.
speaker: CAPULET

text: Of honourable reckoning are you both, And pity 'tis you liv'd at odds so long. But now, my lord, what say you to my suit?
speaker: PAR

text: But saying o'er what I have said before: My child is yet a stranger in the world, She hath not seen the change of fourteen years; Let two more summers wither in their pride Ere we may think her ripe to be a bride.
speaker: CAPULET

text: Younger than she are happy mothers made.
speaker: PAR

text: And too soon marr'd are those so early made. The earth hath swallowed all my hopes but she; She is the hopeful lady of my earth. But woo her, gentle Paris, get her heart; My will to her consent is but a part. An she agree, within her scope of choice Lies my consent and fair according voice. This night I hold an old accustom'd feast, Whereto I have invited many a guest, Such as I love; and you among the store, One more, most welcome, makes my number more. At my poor house look to behold this night Earth-treading stars that make dark heaven light. Such comfort as do lusty young men feel When well apparell'd April on the heel Of limping Winter treads, even such delight Among fresh female buds shall you this night Inherit at my house. Hear all, all see, And like her most whose merit most shall be; Which, on more view of many, mine, being one, May stand in number, though in reck'ning none. Come, go with me. [To Servant, giving him a paper] Go, sirrah, trudge about Through fair Verona; find those persons out Whose names are written there, and to them say, My house and welcome on their pleasure stay-
speaker: CAPULET

text: Exeunt [Capulet and Paris].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Find them out whose names are written here? It is written that the shoemaker should meddle with his yard and the tailor with his last, the fisher with his pencil and the painter with his nets; but I am sent to find those persons whose names are here writ, and can never find what names the writing person hath here writ. I must to the learned. In good time!
speaker: SERV

text: Enter Benvolio and Romeo.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Tut, man, one fire burns out another's burning; One pain is lessened by another's anguish; Turn giddy, and be holp by backward turning; One desperate grief cures with another's languish. Take thou some new infection to thy eye, And the rank poison of the old will die.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Your plantain leaf is excellent for that.
speaker: ROMEO

text: For what, I pray thee?
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: For your broken shin.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Why, Romeo, art thou mad?
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Not mad, but bound more than a madman is; Shut up in prison, kept without my food, Whipp'd and tormented and- God-den, good fellow.
speaker: ROMEO

text: God gi' go-den. I pray, sir, can you read?
speaker: SERV

text: Ay, mine own fortune in my misery.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Perhaps you have learned it without book. But I pray, can you read anything you see?
speaker: SERV

text: Ay, If I know the letters and the language.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Ye say honestly. Rest you merry!
speaker: SERV

text: Stay, fellow; I can read.                       He reads. 'Signior Martino and his wife and daughters; County Anselmo and his beauteous sisters; The lady widow of Vitruvio; Signior Placentio and His lovely nieces; Mercutio and his brother Valentine; Mine uncle Capulet, his wife, and daughters; My fair niece Rosaline and Livia; Signior Valentio and his cousin Tybalt; Lucio and the lively Helena.' [Gives back the paper.] A fair assembly. Whither should they come?
speaker: ROMEO

text: Up.
speaker: SERV

text: Whither?
speaker: ROMEO

text: To supper, to our house.
speaker: SERV

text: Whose house?
speaker: ROMEO

text: My master's.
speaker: SERV

text: Indeed I should have ask'd you that before.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Now I'll tell you without asking. My master is the great rich Capulet; and if you be not of the house of Montagues, I pray come and crush a cup of wine. Rest you merry!               Exit.
speaker: SERV

text: At this same ancient feast of Capulet's Sups the fair Rosaline whom thou so lov'st; With all the admired beauties of Verona. Go thither, and with unattainted eye Compare her face with some that I shall show, And I will make thee think thy swan a crow.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: When the devout religion of mine eye Maintains such falsehood, then turn tears to fires; And these, who, often drown'd, could never die, Transparent heretics, be burnt for liars! One fairer than my love? The all-seeing sun Ne'er saw her match since first the world begun.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Tut! you saw her fair, none else being by, Herself pois'd with herself in either eye; But in that crystal scales let there be weigh'd Your lady's love against some other maid That I will show you shining at this feast, And she shall scant show well that now seems best.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: I'll go along, no such sight to be shown, But to rejoice in splendour of my own.              [Exeunt.]
speaker: ROMEO

text: Enter Capulet's Wife, and Nurse.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Nurse, where's my daughter? Call her forth to me.
speaker: WIFE

text: Now, by my maidenhead at twelve year old, I bade her come. What, lamb! what ladybird! God forbid! Where's this girl? What, Juliet!
speaker: NURSE

text: Enter Juliet.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: How now? Who calls?
speaker: JULIET

text: Your mother.
speaker: NURSE

text: Madam, I am here. What is your will?
speaker: JULIET

text: This is the matter- Nurse, give leave awhile, We must talk in secret. Nurse, come back again; I have rememb'red me, thou's hear our counsel. Thou knowest my daughter's of a pretty age.
speaker: WIFE

text: Faith, I can tell her age unto an hour.
speaker: NURSE

text: She's not fourteen.
speaker: WIFE

text: I'll lay fourteen of my teeth- And yet, to my teen be it spoken, I have but four- She is not fourteen. How long is it now To Lammastide?
speaker: NURSE

text: A fortnight and odd days.
speaker: WIFE

text: Even or odd, of all days in the year, Come Lammas Eve at night shall she be fourteen. Susan and she (God rest all Christian souls!) Were of an age. Well, Susan is with God; She was too good for me. But, as I said, On Lammas Eve at night shall she be fourteen; That shall she, marry; I remember it well. 'Tis since the earthquake now eleven years; And she was wean'd (I never shall forget it), Of all the days of the year, upon that day; For I had then laid wormwood to my dug, Sitting in the sun under the dovehouse wall. My lord and you were then at Mantua. Nay, I do bear a brain. But, as I said, When it did taste the wormwood on the nipple Of my dug and felt it bitter, pretty fool, To see it tetchy and fall out with the dug! Shake, quoth the dovehouse! 'Twas no need, I trow, To bid me trudge. And since that time it is eleven years, For then she could stand alone; nay, by th' rood, She could have run and waddled all about; For even the day before, she broke her brow; And then my husband (God be with his soul! 'A was a merry man) took up the child. 'Yea,' quoth he, 'dost thou fall upon thy face? Thou wilt fall backward when thou hast more wit; Wilt thou not, Jule?' and, by my holidam, The pretty wretch left crying, and said 'Ay.' To see now how a jest shall come about! I warrant, an I should live a thousand yeas, I never should forget it. 'Wilt thou not, Jule?' quoth he, And, pretty fool, it stinted, and said 'Ay.'
speaker: NURSE

text: Enough of this. I pray thee hold thy peace.
speaker: WIFE

text: Yes, madam. Yet I cannot choose but laugh To think it should leave crying and say 'Ay.' And yet, I warrant, it had upon its brow A bump as big as a young cock'rel's stone; A perilous knock; and it cried bitterly. 'Yea,' quoth my husband, 'fall'st upon thy face? Thou wilt fall backward when thou comest to age; Wilt thou not, Jule?' It stinted, and said 'Ay.'
speaker: NURSE

text: And stint thou too, I pray thee, nurse, say I.
speaker: JULIET

text: Peace, I have done. God mark thee to his grace! Thou wast the prettiest babe that e'er I nurs'd. An I might live to see thee married once, I have my wish.
speaker: NURSE

text: Marry, that 'marry' is the very theme I came to talk of. Tell me, daughter Juliet, How stands your disposition to be married?
speaker: WIFE

text: It is an honour that I dream not of.
speaker: JULIET

text: An honour? Were not I thine only nurse, I would say thou hadst suck'd wisdom from thy teat.
speaker: NURSE

text: Well, think of marriage now. Younger than you, Here in Verona, ladies of esteem, Are made already mothers. By my count, I was your mother much upon these years That you are now a maid. Thus then in brief: The valiant Paris seeks you for his love.
speaker: WIFE

text: A man, young lady! lady, such a man As all the world- why he's a man of wax.
speaker: NURSE

text: Verona's summer hath not such a flower.
speaker: WIFE

text: Nay, he's a flower, in faith- a very flower.
speaker: NURSE

text: What say you? Can you love the gentleman? This night you shall behold him at our feast. Read o'er the volume of young Paris' face, And find delight writ there with beauty's pen; Examine every married lineament, And see how one another lends content; And what obscur'd in this fair volume lies Find written in the margent of his eyes, This precious book of love, this unbound lover, To beautify him only lacks a cover. The fish lives in the sea, and 'tis much pride For fair without the fair within to hide. That book in many's eyes doth share the glory, That in gold clasps locks in the golden story; So shall you share all that he doth possess, By having him making yourself no less.
speaker: WIFE

text: No less? Nay, bigger! Women grow by men
speaker: NURSE

text: Speak briefly, can you like of Paris' love?
speaker: WIFE

text: I'll look to like, if looking liking move; But no more deep will I endart mine eye Than your consent gives strength to make it fly.
speaker: JULIET

text: Enter Servingman.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Madam, the guests are come, supper serv'd up, you call'd, my young lady ask'd for, the nurse curs'd in the pantry, and everything in extremity. I must hence to wait. I beseech you follow straight.
speaker: SERV

text: We follow thee.                       Exit [Servingman]. Juliet, the County stays.
speaker: WIFE

text: Go, girl, seek happy nights to happy days.
speaker: NURSE

text: Exeunt.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter Romeo, Mercutio, Benvolio, with five or six other Maskers;
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Torchbearers.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: What, shall this speech be spoke for our excuse? Or shall we on without apology?
speaker: ROMEO

text: The date is out of such prolixity. We'll have no Cupid hoodwink'd with a scarf, Bearing a Tartar's painted bow of lath, Scaring the ladies like a crowkeeper; Nor no without-book prologue, faintly spoke After the prompter, for our entrance; But, let them measure us by what they will, We'll measure them a measure, and be gone.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Give me a torch. I am not for this ambling. Being but heavy, I will bear the light.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Nay, gentle Romeo, we must have you dance.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Not I, believe me. You have dancing shoes With nimble soles; I have a soul of lead So stakes me to the ground I cannot move.
speaker: ROMEO

text: You are a lover. Borrow Cupid's wings And soar with them above a common bound.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: I am too sore enpierced with his shaft To soar with his light feathers; and so bound I cannot bound a pitch above dull woe. Under love's heavy burthen do I sink.
speaker: ROMEO

text: And, to sink in it, should you burthen love- Too great oppression for a tender thing.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Is love a tender thing? It is too rough, Too rude, too boist'rous, and it pricks like thorn.
speaker: ROMEO

text: If love be rough with you, be rough with love. Prick love for pricking, and you beat love down. Give me a case to put my visage in. A visor for a visor! What care I What curious eye doth quote deformities? Here are the beetle brows shall blush for me.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Come, knock and enter; and no sooner in But every man betake him to his legs.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: A torch for me! Let wantons light of heart Tickle the senseless rushes with their heels; For I am proverb'd with a grandsire phrase, I'll be a candle-holder and look on; The game was ne'er so fair, and I am done.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Tut! dun's the mouse, the constable's own word! If thou art Dun, we'll draw thee from the mire Of this sir-reverence love, wherein thou stick'st Up to the ears. Come, we burn daylight, ho!
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Nay, that's not so.
speaker: ROMEO

text: I mean, sir, in delay We waste our lights in vain, like lamps by day. Take our good meaning, for our judgment sits Five times in that ere once in our five wits.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: And we mean well, in going to this masque; But 'tis no wit to go.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Why, may one ask?
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: I dreamt a dream to-night.
speaker: ROMEO

text: And so did I.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Well, what was yours?
speaker: ROMEO

text: That dreamers often lie.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: In bed asleep, while they do dream things true.
speaker: ROMEO

text: O, then I see Queen Mab hath been with you. She is the fairies' midwife, and she comes In shape no bigger than an agate stone On the forefinger of an alderman, Drawn with a team of little atomies Athwart men's noses as they lie asleep; Her wagon spokes made of long spinners' legs, The cover, of the wings of grasshoppers; Her traces, of the smallest spider's web; Her collars, of the moonshine's wat'ry beams; Her whip, of cricket's bone; the lash, of film; Her wagoner, a small grey-coated gnat, Not half so big as a round little worm Prick'd from the lazy finger of a maid; Her chariot is an empty hazelnut, Made by the joiner squirrel or old grub, Time out o' mind the fairies' coachmakers. And in this state she gallops night by night Through lovers' brains, and then they dream of love; O'er courtiers' knees, that dream on cursies straight; O'er lawyers' fingers, who straight dream on fees; O'er ladies' lips, who straight on kisses dream, Which oft the angry Mab with blisters plagues, Because their breaths with sweetmeats tainted are. Sometime she gallops o'er a courtier's nose, And then dreams he of smelling out a suit; And sometime comes she with a tithe-pig's tail Tickling a parson's nose as 'a lies asleep, Then dreams he of another benefice. Sometimes she driveth o'er a soldier's neck, And then dreams he of cutting foreign throats, Of breaches, ambuscadoes, Spanish blades, Of healths five fadom deep; and then anon Drums in his ear, at which he starts and wakes, And being thus frighted, swears a prayer or two And sleeps again. This is that very Mab That plats the manes of horses in the night And bakes the elflocks in foul sluttish, hairs, Which once untangled much misfortune bodes This is the hag, when maids lie on their backs, That presses them and learns them first to bear, Making them women of good carriage. This is she-
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Peace, peace, Mercutio, peace! Thou talk'st of nothing.
speaker: ROMEO

text: True, I talk of dreams; Which are the children of an idle brain, Begot of nothing but vain fantasy; Which is as thin of substance as the air, And more inconstant than the wind, who wooes Even now the frozen bosom of the North And, being anger'd, puffs away from thence, Turning his face to the dew-dropping South.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: This wind you talk of blows us from ourselves. Supper is done, and we shall come too late.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: I fear, too early; for my mind misgives Some consequence, yet hanging in the stars, Shall bitterly begin his fearful date With this night's revels and expire the term Of a despised life, clos'd in my breast, By some vile forfeit of untimely death. But he that hath the steerage of my course Direct my sail! On, lusty gentlemen!
speaker: ROMEO

text: Strike, drum. They march about the stage. [Exeunt.]
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Enter the Maskers, Enter, [with Servants,] Capulet, his Wife,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Juliet, Tybalt, and all the Guests
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: and Gentlewomen to the Maskers.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Welcome, gentlemen! Ladies that have their toes Unplagu'd with corns will have a bout with you. Ah ha, my mistresses! which of you all Will now deny to dance? She that makes dainty, She I'll swear hath corns. Am I come near ye now? Welcome, gentlemen! I have seen the day That I have worn a visor and could tell A whispering tale in a fair lady's ear, Such as would please. 'Tis gone, 'tis gone, 'tis gone! You are welcome, gentlemen! Come, musicians, play. A hall, a hall! give room! and foot it, girls. Music plays, and they dance. More light, you knaves! and turn the tables up, And quench the fire, the room is grown too hot. Ah, sirrah, this unlook'd-for sport comes well. Nay, sit, nay, sit, good cousin Capulet, For you and I are past our dancing days. How long is't now since last yourself and I Were in a mask? 2. Cap. By'r Lady, thirty years.
speaker: CAPULET

text: What, man? 'Tis not so much, 'tis not so much! 'Tis since the nuptial of Lucentio, Come Pentecost as quickly as it will, Some five-and-twenty years, and then we mask'd. 2. Cap. 'Tis more, 'tis more! His son is elder, sir; His son is thirty.
speaker: CAPULET

text: Will you tell me that? His son was but a ward two years ago.
speaker: CAPULET

text: [to a Servingman] What lady's that, which doth enrich the hand Of yonder knight?
speaker: ROMEO

text: I know not, sir.
speaker: SERV

text: O, she doth teach the torches to burn bright! It seems she hangs upon the cheek of night Like a rich jewel in an Ethiop's ear- Beauty too rich for use, for earth too dear! So shows a snowy dove trooping with crows As yonder lady o'er her fellows shows. The measure done, I'll watch her place of stand And, touching hers, make blessed my rude hand. Did my heart love till now? Forswear it, sight! For I ne'er saw true beauty till this night.
speaker: ROMEO

text: This, by his voice, should be a Montague. Fetch me my rapier, boy. What, dares the slave Come hither, cover'd with an antic face, To fleer and scorn at our solemnity? Now, by the stock and honour of my kin, To strike him dead I hold it not a sin.
speaker: TYBALT

text: Why, how now, kinsman? Wherefore storm you so?
speaker: CAPULET

text: Uncle, this is a Montague, our foe; A villain, that is hither come in spite To scorn at our solemnity this night.
speaker: TYBALT

text: Young Romeo is it?
speaker: CAPULET

text: 'Tis he, that villain Romeo.
speaker: TYBALT

text: Content thee, gentle coz, let him alone. 'A bears him like a portly gentleman, And, to say truth, Verona brags of him To be a virtuous and well-govern'd youth. I would not for the wealth of all this town Here in my house do him disparagement. Therefore be patient, take no note of him. It is my will; the which if thou respect, Show a fair presence and put off these frowns, An ill-beseeming semblance for a feast.
speaker: CAPULET

text: It fits when such a villain is a guest. I'll not endure him.
speaker: TYBALT

text: He shall be endur'd. What, goodman boy? I say he shall. Go to! Am I the master here, or you? Go to! You'll not endure him? God shall mend my soul! You'll make a mutiny among my guests! You will set cock-a-hoop! you'll be the man!
speaker: CAPULET

text: Why, uncle, 'tis a shame.
speaker: TYBALT

text: Go to, go to! You are a saucy boy. Is't so, indeed? This trick may chance to scathe you. I know what. You must contrary me! Marry, 'tis time.- Well said, my hearts!- You are a princox- go! Be quiet, or- More light, more light!- For shame! I'll make you quiet; what!- Cheerly, my hearts!
speaker: CAPULET

text: Patience perforce with wilful choler meeting Makes my flesh tremble in their different greeting. I will withdraw; but this intrusion shall, Now seeming sweet, convert to bitt'rest gall.          Exit.
speaker: TYBALT

text: If I profane with my unworthiest hand This holy shrine, the gentle fine is this: My lips, two blushing pilgrims, ready stand To smooth that rough touch with a tender kiss.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Good pilgrim, you do wrong your hand too much, Which mannerly devotion shows in this; For saints have hands that pilgrims' hands do touch, And palm to palm is holy palmers' kiss.
speaker: JULIET

text: Have not saints lips, and holy palmers too?
speaker: ROMEO

text: Ay, pilgrim, lips that they must use in pray'r.
speaker: JULIET

text: O, then, dear saint, let lips do what hands do! They pray; grant thou, lest faith turn to despair.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Saints do not move, though grant for prayers' sake.
speaker: JULIET

text: Then move not while my prayer's effect I take. Thus from my lips, by thine my sin is purg'd.  [Kisses her.]
speaker: ROMEO

text: Then have my lips the sin that they have took.
speaker: JULIET

text: Sin from my lips? O trespass sweetly urg'd! Give me my sin again.                          [Kisses her.]
speaker: ROMEO

text: You kiss by th' book.
speaker: JULIET

text: Madam, your mother craves a word with you.
speaker: NURSE

text: What is her mother?
speaker: ROMEO

text: Marry, bachelor, Her mother is the lady of the house. And a good lady, and a wise and virtuous. I nurs'd her daughter that you talk'd withal. I tell you, he that can lay hold of her Shall have the chinks.
speaker: NURSE

text: Is she a Capulet? O dear account! my life is my foe's debt.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Away, be gone; the sport is at the best.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Ay, so I fear; the more is my unrest.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Nay, gentlemen, prepare not to be gone; We have a trifling foolish banquet towards. Is it e'en so? Why then, I thank you all. I thank you, honest gentlemen. Good night. More torches here! [Exeunt Maskers.] Come on then, let's to bed. Ah, sirrah, by my fay, it waxes late; I'll to my rest.
speaker: CAPULET

text: Exeunt [all but Juliet and Nurse].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Come hither, nurse. What is yond gentleman?
speaker: JULIET

text: The son and heir of old Tiberio.
speaker: NURSE

text: What's he that now is going out of door?
speaker: JULIET

text: Marry, that, I think, be young Petruchio.
speaker: NURSE

text: What's he that follows there, that would not dance?
speaker: JULIET

text: I know not.
speaker: NURSE

text: Go ask his name.- If he be married, My grave is like to be my wedding bed.
speaker: JULIET

text: His name is Romeo, and a Montague, The only son of your great enemy.
speaker: NURSE

text: My only love, sprung from my only hate! Too early seen unknown, and known too late! Prodigious birth of love it is to me That I must love a loathed enemy.
speaker: JULIET

text: What's this? what's this?
speaker: NURSE

text: A rhyme I learnt even now Of one I danc'd withal. One calls within, 'Juliet.'
speaker: JULIET

text: Anon, anon! Come, let's away; the strangers all are gone.        Exeunt. SHAKESPEARE IS COPYRIGHT 1990-1993 BY WORLD LIBRARY, INC., AND IS

text: Enter Chorus.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Now old desire doth in his deathbed lie, And young affection gapes to be his heir; That fair for which love groan'd for and would die, With tender Juliet match'd, is now not fair. Now Romeo is belov'd, and loves again, Alike bewitched by the charm of looks; But to his foe suppos'd he must complain, And she steal love's sweet bait from fearful hooks. Being held a foe, he may not have access To breathe such vows as lovers use to swear, And she as much in love, her means much less To meet her new beloved anywhere; But passion lends them power, time means, to meet, Temp'ring extremities with extreme sweet.
speaker: CHORUS

text: Exit.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter Romeo alone.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Can I go forward when my heart is here? Turn back, dull earth, and find thy centre out.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Climbs the wall and leaps down within it.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter Benvolio with Mercutio.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Romeo! my cousin Romeo! Romeo!
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: He is wise, And, on my life, hath stol'n him home to bed.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: He ran this way, and leapt this orchard wall. Call, good Mercutio.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Nay, I'll conjure too. Romeo! humours! madman! passion! lover! Appear thou in the likeness of a sigh; Speak but one rhyme, and I am satisfied! Cry but 'Ah me!' pronounce but 'love' and 'dove'; Speak to my gossip Venus one fair word, One nickname for her purblind son and heir, Young auburn Cupid, he that shot so trim When King Cophetua lov'd the beggar maid! He heareth not, he stirreth not, be moveth not; The ape is dead, and I must conjure him. I conjure thee by Rosaline's bright eyes. By her high forehead and her scarlet lip, By her fine foot, straight leg, and quivering thigh, And the demesnes that there adjacent lie, That in thy likeness thou appear to us!
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: An if he hear thee, thou wilt anger him.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: This cannot anger him. 'Twould anger him To raise a spirit in his mistress' circle Of some strange nature, letting it there stand Till she had laid it and conjur'd it down. That were some spite; my invocation Is fair and honest: in his mistress' name, I conjure only but to raise up him.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Come, he hath hid himself among these trees To be consorted with the humorous night. Blind is his love and best befits the dark.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: If love be blind, love cannot hit the mark. Now will he sit under a medlar tree And wish his mistress were that kind of fruit As maids call medlars when they laugh alone. O, Romeo, that she were, O that she were An open et cetera, thou a pop'rin pear! Romeo, good night. I'll to my truckle-bed; This field-bed is too cold for me to sleep. Come, shall we go?
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Go then, for 'tis in vain 'To seek him here that means not to be found.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Exeunt.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter Romeo.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: He jests at scars that never felt a wound.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Enter Juliet above at a window.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: But soft! What light through yonder window breaks?
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: It is the East, and Juliet is the sun!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Arise, fair sun, and kill the envious moon,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Who is already sick and pale with grief
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: That thou her maid art far more fair than she.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Be not her maid, since she is envious.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Her vestal livery is but sick and green,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: And none but fools do wear it. Cast it off.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: It is my lady; O, it is my love!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: O that she knew she were!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: She speaks, yet she says nothing. What of that?
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Her eye discourses; I will answer it.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: I am too bold; 'tis not to me she speaks.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Two of the fairest stars in all the heaven,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Having some business, do entreat her eyes
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: To twinkle in their spheres till they return.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: What if her eyes were there, they in her head?
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: The brightness of her cheek would shame those stars
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: As daylight doth a lamp; her eyes in heaven
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Would through the airy region stream so bright
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: That birds would sing and think it were not night.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: See how she leans her cheek upon her hand!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: O that I were a glove upon that hand,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: That I might touch that cheek!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Ah me!
speaker: JULIET

text: She speaks. O, speak again, bright angel! for thou art As glorious to this night, being o'er my head, As is a winged messenger of heaven Unto the white-upturned wond'ring eyes Of mortals that fall back to gaze on him When he bestrides the lazy-pacing clouds And sails upon the bosom of the air.
speaker: ROMEO

text: O Romeo, Romeo! wherefore art thou Romeo? Deny thy father and refuse thy name! Or, if thou wilt not, be but sworn my love, And I'll no longer be a Capulet.
speaker: JULIET

text: [aside] Shall I hear more, or shall I speak at this?
speaker: ROMEO

text: 'Tis but thy name that is my enemy. Thou art thyself, though not a Montague. What's Montague? it is nor hand, nor foot, Nor arm, nor face, nor any other part Belonging to a man. O, be some other name! What's in a name? That which we call a rose By any other name would smell as sweet. So Romeo would, were he not Romeo call'd, Retain that dear perfection which he owes Without that title. Romeo, doff thy name; And for that name, which is no part of thee, Take all myself.
speaker: JULIET

text: I take thee at thy word. Call me but love, and I'll be new baptiz'd; Henceforth I never will be Romeo.
speaker: ROMEO

text: What man art thou that, thus bescreen'd in night, So stumblest on my counsel?
speaker: JULIET

text: By a name I know not how to tell thee who I am. My name, dear saint, is hateful to myself, Because it is an enemy to thee. Had I it written, I would tear the word.
speaker: ROMEO

text: My ears have yet not drunk a hundred words Of that tongue's utterance, yet I know the sound. Art thou not Romeo, and a Montague?
speaker: JULIET

text: Neither, fair saint, if either thee dislike.
speaker: ROMEO

text: How cam'st thou hither, tell me, and wherefore? The orchard walls are high and hard to climb, And the place death, considering who thou art, If any of my kinsmen find thee here.
speaker: JULIET

text: With love's light wings did I o'erperch these walls; For stony limits cannot hold love out, And what love can do, that dares love attempt. Therefore thy kinsmen are no let to me.
speaker: ROMEO

text: If they do see thee, they will murther thee.
speaker: JULIET

text: Alack, there lies more peril in thine eye Than twenty of their swords! Look thou but sweet, And I am proof against their enmity.
speaker: ROMEO

text: I would not for the world they saw thee here.
speaker: JULIET

text: I have night's cloak to hide me from their sight; And but thou love me, let them find me here. My life were better ended by their hate Than death prorogued, wanting of thy love.
speaker: ROMEO

text: By whose direction found'st thou out this place?
speaker: JULIET

text: By love, that first did prompt me to enquire. He lent me counsel, and I lent him eyes. I am no pilot; yet, wert thou as far As that vast shore wash'd with the farthest sea, I would adventure for such merchandise.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Thou knowest the mask of night is on my face; Else would a maiden blush bepaint my cheek For that which thou hast heard me speak to-night. Fain would I dwell on form- fain, fain deny What I have spoke; but farewell compliment! Dost thou love me, I know thou wilt say 'Ay'; And I will take thy word. Yet, if thou swear'st, Thou mayst prove false. At lovers' perjuries, They say Jove laughs. O gentle Romeo, If thou dost love, pronounce it faithfully. Or if thou thinkest I am too quickly won, I'll frown, and be perverse, and say thee nay, So thou wilt woo; but else, not for the world. In truth, fair Montague, I am too fond, And therefore thou mayst think my haviour light; But trust me, gentleman, I'll prove more true Than those that have more cunning to be strange. I should have been more strange, I must confess, But that thou overheard'st, ere I was ware, My true-love passion. Therefore pardon me, And not impute this yielding to light love, Which the dark night hath so discovered.
speaker: JULIET

text: Lady, by yonder blessed moon I swear, That tips with silver all these fruit-tree tops-
speaker: ROMEO

text: O, swear not by the moon, th' inconstant moon, That monthly changes in her circled orb, Lest that thy love prove likewise variable.
speaker: JULIET

text: What shall I swear by?
speaker: ROMEO

text: Do not swear at all; Or if thou wilt, swear by thy gracious self, Which is the god of my idolatry, And I'll believe thee.
speaker: JULIET

text: If my heart's dear love-
speaker: ROMEO

text: Well, do not swear. Although I joy in thee, I have no joy of this contract to-night. It is too rash, too unadvis'd, too sudden; Too like the lightning, which doth cease to be Ere one can say 'It lightens.' Sweet, good night! This bud of love, by summer's ripening breath, May prove a beauteous flow'r when next we meet. Good night, good night! As sweet repose and rest Come to thy heart as that within my breast!
speaker: JULIET

text: O, wilt thou leave me so unsatisfied?
speaker: ROMEO

text: What satisfaction canst thou have to-night?
speaker: JULIET

text: Th' exchange of thy love's faithful vow for mine.
speaker: ROMEO

text: I gave thee mine before thou didst request it; And yet I would it were to give again.
speaker: JULIET

text: Would'st thou withdraw it? For what purpose, love?
speaker: ROMEO

text: But to be frank and give it thee again. And yet I wish but for the thing I have. My bounty is as boundless as the sea, My love as deep; the more I give to thee, The more I have, for both are infinite. I hear some noise within. Dear love, adieu! [Nurse] calls within. Anon, good nurse! Sweet Montague, be true. Stay but a little, I will come again.                [Exit.]
speaker: JULIET

text: O blessed, blessed night! I am afeard, Being in night, all this is but a dream, Too flattering-sweet to be substantial.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Enter Juliet above.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Three words, dear Romeo, and good night indeed. If that thy bent of love be honourable, Thy purpose marriage, send me word to-morrow, By one that I'll procure to come to thee, Where and what time thou wilt perform the rite; And all my fortunes at thy foot I'll lay And follow thee my lord throughout the world.
speaker: JULIET

text: (within) Madam!
speaker: NURSE

text: I come, anon.- But if thou meanest not well, I do beseech thee-
speaker: JULIET

text: (within) Madam!
speaker: NURSE

text: By-and-by I come.- To cease thy suit and leave me to my grief. To-morrow will I send.
speaker: JULIET

text: So thrive my soul-
speaker: ROMEO

text: A thousand times good night!                        Exit.
speaker: JULIET

text: A thousand times the worse, to want thy light! Love goes toward love as schoolboys from their books; But love from love, towards school with heavy looks.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Enter Juliet again, [above].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Hist! Romeo, hist! O for a falconer's voice To lure this tassel-gentle back again! Bondage is hoarse and may not speak aloud; Else would I tear the cave where Echo lies, And make her airy tongue more hoarse than mine With repetition of my Romeo's name. Romeo!
speaker: JULIET

text: It is my soul that calls upon my name. How silver-sweet sound lovers' tongues by night, Like softest music to attending ears!
speaker: ROMEO

text: Romeo!
speaker: JULIET

text: My dear?
speaker: ROMEO

text: At what o'clock to-morrow Shall I send to thee?
speaker: JULIET

text: By the hour of nine.
speaker: ROMEO

text: I will not fail. 'Tis twenty years till then. I have forgot why I did call thee back.
speaker: JULIET

text: Let me stand here till thou remember it.
speaker: ROMEO

text: I shall forget, to have thee still stand there, Rememb'ring how I love thy company.
speaker: JULIET

text: And I'll still stay, to have thee still forget, Forgetting any other home but this.
speaker: ROMEO

text: 'Tis almost morning. I would have thee gone- And yet no farther than a wanton's bird, That lets it hop a little from her hand, Like a poor prisoner in his twisted gyves, And with a silk thread plucks it back again, So loving-jealous of his liberty.
speaker: JULIET

text: I would I were thy bird.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Sweet, so would I. Yet I should kill thee with much cherishing. Good night, good night! Parting is such sweet sorrow, That I shall say good night till it be morrow.
speaker: JULIET

text: Exit.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Sleep dwell upon thine eyes, peace in thy breast! Would I were sleep and peace, so sweet to rest! Hence will I to my ghostly father's cell, His help to crave and my dear hap to tell.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Exit
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter Friar, [Laurence] alone, with a basket.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: The grey-ey'd morn smiles on the frowning night, Check'ring the Eastern clouds with streaks of light; And flecked darkness like a drunkard reels From forth day's path and Titan's fiery wheels. Non, ere the sun advance his burning eye The day to cheer and night's dank dew to dry, I must up-fill this osier cage of ours With baleful weeds and precious-juiced flowers. The earth that's nature's mother is her tomb. What is her burying gave, that is her womb; And from her womb children of divers kind We sucking on her natural bosom find; Many for many virtues excellent, None but for some, and yet all different. O, mickle is the powerful grace that lies In plants, herbs, stones, and their true qualities; For naught so vile that on the earth doth live But to the earth some special good doth give; Nor aught so good but, strain'd from that fair use, Revolts from true birth, stumbling on abuse. Virtue itself turns vice, being misapplied, And vice sometime's by action dignified. Within the infant rind of this small flower Poison hath residence, and medicine power; For this, being smelt, with that part cheers each part; Being tasted, slays all senses with the heart. Two such opposed kings encamp them still In man as well as herbs- grace and rude will; And where the worser is predominant, Full soon the canker death eats up that plant.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: Enter Romeo.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Good morrow, father.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Benedicite! What early tongue so sweet saluteth me? Young son, it argues a distempered head So soon to bid good morrow to thy bed. Care keeps his watch in every old man's eye, And where care lodges sleep will never lie; But where unbruised youth with unstuff'd brain Doth couch his limbs, there golden sleep doth reign. Therefore thy earliness doth me assure Thou art uprous'd with some distemp'rature; Or if not so, then here I hit it right- Our Romeo hath not been in bed to-night.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: That last is true-the sweeter rest was mine.
speaker: ROMEO

text: God pardon sin! Wast thou with Rosaline?
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: With Rosaline, my ghostly father? No. I have forgot that name, and that name's woe.
speaker: ROMEO

text: That's my good son! But where hast thou been then?
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: I'll tell thee ere thou ask it me again. I have been feasting with mine enemy, Where on a sudden one hath wounded me That's by me wounded. Both our remedies Within thy help and holy physic lies. I bear no hatred, blessed man, for, lo, My intercession likewise steads my foe.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Be plain, good son, and homely in thy drift Riddling confession finds but riddling shrift.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: Then plainly know my heart's dear love is set On the fair daughter of rich Capulet; As mine on hers, so hers is set on mine, And all combin'd, save what thou must combine By holy marriage. When, and where, and how We met, we woo'd, and made exchange of vow, I'll tell thee as we pass; but this I pray, That thou consent to marry us to-day.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Holy Saint Francis! What a change is here! Is Rosaline, that thou didst love so dear, So soon forsaken? Young men's love then lies Not truly in their hearts, but in their eyes. Jesu Maria! What a deal of brine Hath wash'd thy sallow cheeks for Rosaline! How much salt water thrown away in waste, To season love, that of it doth not taste! The sun not yet thy sighs from heaven clears, Thy old groans ring yet in mine ancient ears. Lo, here upon thy cheek the stain doth sit Of an old tear that is not wash'd off yet. If e'er thou wast thyself, and these woes thine, Thou and these woes were all for Rosaline. And art thou chang'd? Pronounce this sentence then: Women may fall when there's no strength in men.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: Thou chid'st me oft for loving Rosaline.
speaker: ROMEO

text: For doting, not for loving, pupil mine.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: And bad'st me bury love.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Not in a grave To lay one in, another out to have.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: I pray thee chide not. She whom I love now Doth grace for grace and love for love allow. The other did not so.
speaker: ROMEO

text: O, she knew well Thy love did read by rote, that could not spell. But come, young waverer, come go with me. In one respect I'll thy assistant be; For this alliance may so happy prove To turn your households' rancour to pure love.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: O, let us hence! I stand on sudden haste.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Wisely, and slow. They stumble that run fast.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: Exeunt.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter Benvolio and Mercutio.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Where the devil should this Romeo be? Came he not home to-night?
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Not to his father's. I spoke with his man.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Why, that same pale hard-hearted wench, that Rosaline, Torments him so that he will sure run mad.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Tybalt, the kinsman to old Capulet, Hath sent a letter to his father's house.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: A challenge, on my life.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Romeo will answer it.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Any man that can write may answer a letter.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Nay, he will answer the letter's master, how he dares, being dared.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Alas, poor Romeo, he is already dead! stabb'd with a white wench's black eye; shot through the ear with a love song; the very pin of his heart cleft with the blind bow-boy's butt-shaft; and is he a man to encounter Tybalt?
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Why, what is Tybalt?
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: More than Prince of Cats, I can tell you. O, he's the courageous captain of compliments. He fights as you sing pricksong-keeps time, distance, and proportion; rests me his minim rest, one, two, and the third in your bosom! the very butcher of a silk button, a duellist, a duellist! a gentleman of the very first house, of the first and second cause. Ah, the immortal passado! the punto reverse! the hay.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: The what?
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: The pox of such antic, lisping, affecting fantasticoes- these new tuners of accent! 'By Jesu, a very good blade! a very tall man! a very good whore!' Why, is not this a lamentable thing, grandsir, that we should be thus afflicted with these strange flies, these fashion-mongers, these pardona-mi's, who stand so much on the new form that they cannot sit at ease on the old bench? O, their bones, their bones!
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Enter Romeo.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Here comes Romeo! here comes Romeo!
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Without his roe, like a dried herring. O flesh, flesh, how art thou fishified! Now is he for the numbers that Petrarch flowed in. Laura, to his lady, was but a kitchen wench (marry, she had a better love to berhyme her), Dido a dowdy, Cleopatra a gypsy, Helen and Hero hildings and harlots, This be a gray eye or so, but not to the purpose. Signior Romeo, bon jour! There's a French salutation to your French slop. You gave us the counterfeit fairly last night.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Good morrow to you both. What counterfeit did I give you?
speaker: ROMEO

text: The slip, sir, the slip. Can you not conceive?
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Pardon, good Mercutio. My business was great, and in such a case as mine a man may strain courtesy.
speaker: ROMEO

text: That's as much as to say, such a case as yours constrains a man to bow in the hams.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Meaning, to cursy.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Thou hast most kindly hit it.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: A most courteous exposition.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Nay, I am the very pink of courtesy.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Pink for flower.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Right.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Why, then is my pump well-flower'd.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Well said! Follow me this jest now till thou hast worn out thy pump, that, when the single sole of it is worn, the jest may remain, after the wearing, solely singular.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: O single-sold jest, solely singular for the singleness!
speaker: ROMEO

text: Come between us, good Benvolio! My wits faint.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Swits and spurs, swits and spurs! or I'll cry a match.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Nay, if our wits run the wild-goose chase, I am done; for thou hast more of the wild goose in one of thy wits than, I am sure, I have in my whole five. Was I with you there for the goose?
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Thou wast never with me for anything when thou wast not there for the goose.
speaker: ROMEO

text: I will bite thee by the ear for that jest.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Nay, good goose, bite not!
speaker: ROMEO

text: Thy wit is a very bitter sweeting; it is a most sharp sauce.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: And is it not, then, well serv'd in to a sweet goose?
speaker: ROMEO

text: O, here's a wit of cheveril, that stretches from an inch narrow to an ell broad!
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: I stretch it out for that word 'broad,' which, added to the goose, proves thee far and wide a broad goose.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Why, is not this better now than groaning for love? Now art thou sociable, now art thou Romeo; now art thou what thou art, by art as well as by nature. For this drivelling love is like a great natural that runs lolling up and down to hide his bauble in a hole.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Stop there, stop there!
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Thou desirest me to stop in my tale against the hair.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Thou wouldst else have made thy tale large.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: O, thou art deceiv'd! I would have made it short; for I was come to the whole depth of my tale, and meant indeed to occupy the argument no longer.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Here's goodly gear!
speaker: ROMEO

text: Enter Nurse and her Man [Peter].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: A sail, a sail!
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Two, two! a shirt and a smock.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: Peter!
speaker: NURSE

text: Anon.
speaker: PETER

text: My fan, Peter.
speaker: NURSE

text: Good Peter, to hide her face; for her fan's the fairer face of the two.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: God ye good morrow, gentlemen.
speaker: NURSE

text: God ye good-den, fair gentlewoman.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Is it good-den?
speaker: NURSE

text: 'Tis no less, I tell ye; for the bawdy hand of the dial is now upon the prick of noon.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Out upon you! What a man are you!
speaker: NURSE

text: One, gentlewoman, that God hath made for himself to mar.
speaker: ROMEO

text: By my troth, it is well said. 'For himself to mar,' quoth 'a? Gentlemen, can any of you tell me where I may find the young Romeo?
speaker: NURSE

text: I can tell you; but young Romeo will be older when you have found him than he was when you sought him. I am the youngest of that name, for fault of a worse.
speaker: ROMEO

text: You say well.
speaker: NURSE

text: Yea, is the worst well? Very well took, i' faith! wisely, wisely.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: If you be he, sir, I desire some confidence with you.
speaker: NURSE

text: She will endite him to some supper.
speaker: BENVOLIO

text: A bawd, a bawd, a bawd! So ho!
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: What hast thou found?
speaker: ROMEO

text: No hare, sir; unless a hare, sir, in a lenten pie, that is something stale and hoar ere it be spent He walks by them and sings. An old hare hoar, And an old hare hoar, Is very good meat in Lent; But a hare that is hoar Is too much for a score When it hoars ere it be spent. Romeo, will you come to your father's? We'll to dinner thither.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: I will follow you.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Farewell, ancient lady. Farewell, [sings] lady, lady, lady.
speaker: MERCUTIO

text: Exeunt Mercutio, Benvolio.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Marry, farewell! I Pray you, Sir, what saucy merchant was this that was so full of his ropery?
speaker: NURSE

text: A gentleman, nurse, that loves to hear himself talk and will speak more in a minute than he will stand to in a month.
speaker: ROMEO

text: An 'a speak anything against me, I'll take him down, an 'a were lustier than he is, and twenty such jacks; and if I cannot, I'll find those that shall. Scurvy knave! I am none of his flirt-gills; I am none of his skains-mates. And thou must stand by too, and suffer every knave to use me at his pleasure!
speaker: NURSE

text: I saw no man use you at his pleasure. If I had, my weapon should quickly have been out, I warrant you. I dare draw as soon as another man, if I see occasion in a good quarrel, and the law on my side.
speaker: PETER

text: Now, afore God, I am so vexed that every part about me quivers. Scurvy knave! Pray you, sir, a word; and, as I told you, my young lady bid me enquire you out. What she bid me say, I will keep to myself; but first let me tell ye, if ye should lead her into a fool's paradise, as they say, it were a very gross kind of behaviour, as they say; for the gentlewoman is young; and therefore, if you should deal double with her, truly it were an ill thing to be off'red to any gentlewoman, and very weak dealing.
speaker: NURSE

text: Nurse, commend me to thy lady and mistress. I protest unto thee-
speaker: ROMEO

text: Good heart, and i' faith I will tell her as much. Lord, Lord! she will be a joyful woman.
speaker: NURSE

text: What wilt thou tell her, nurse? Thou dost not mark me.
speaker: ROMEO

text: I will tell her, sir, that you do protest, which, as I take it, is a gentlemanlike offer.
speaker: NURSE

text: Bid her devise Some means to come to shrift this afternoon; And there she shall at Friar Laurence' cell Be shriv'd and married. Here is for thy pains.
speaker: ROMEO

text: No, truly, sir; not a penny.
speaker: NURSE

text: Go to! I say you shall.
speaker: ROMEO

text: This afternoon, sir? Well, she shall be there.
speaker: NURSE

text: And stay, good nurse, behind the abbey wall. Within this hour my man shall be with thee And bring thee cords made like a tackled stair, Which to the high topgallant of my joy Must be my convoy in the secret night.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Be trusty, and I'll quit thy pains.
speaker: FAREWELL

text: Commend me to thy mistress.
speaker: FAREWELL

text: Now God in heaven bless thee! Hark you, sir.
speaker: NURSE

text: What say'st thou, my dear nurse?
speaker: ROMEO

text: Is your man secret? Did you ne'er hear say, Two may keep counsel, putting one away?
speaker: NURSE

text: I warrant thee my man's as true as steel.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Well, sir, my mistress is the sweetest lady. Lord, Lord! when 'twas a little prating thing- O, there is a nobleman in town, one Paris, that would fain lay knife aboard; but she, good soul, had as lieve see a toad, a very toad, as see him. I anger her sometimes, and tell her that Paris is the properer man; but I'll warrant you, when I say so, she looks as pale as any clout in the versal world. Doth not rosemary and Romeo begin both with a letter?
speaker: NURSE

text: Ay, nurse; what of that? Both with an R.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Ah, mocker! that's the dog's name. R is for the- No; I know it begins with some other letter; and she hath the prettiest sententious of it, of you and rosemary, that it would do you good to hear it.
speaker: NURSE

text: Commend me to thy lady.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Ay, a thousand times. [Exit Romeo.] Peter!
speaker: NURSE

text: Anon.
speaker: PETER

text: Peter, take my fan, and go before, and apace.
speaker: NURSE

text: Exeunt.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter Juliet.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: The clock struck nine when I did send the nurse; In half an hour she promis'd to return. Perchance she cannot meet him. That's not so. O, she is lame! Love's heralds should be thoughts, Which ten times faster glide than the sun's beams Driving back shadows over low'ring hills. Therefore do nimble-pinion'd doves draw Love, And therefore hath the wind-swift Cupid wings. Now is the sun upon the highmost hill Of this day's journey, and from nine till twelve Is three long hours; yet she is not come. Had she affections and warm youthful blood, She would be as swift in motion as a ball; My words would bandy her to my sweet love, And his to me, But old folks, many feign as they were dead- Unwieldy, slow, heavy and pale as lead.
speaker: JULIET

text: Enter Nurse [and Peter].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: O God, she comes! O honey nurse, what news?
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Hast thou met with him? Send thy man away.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Peter, stay at the gate.
speaker: NURSE

text: Exit Peter.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Now, good sweet nurse- O Lord, why look'st thou sad? Though news be sad, yet tell them merrily; If good, thou shamest the music of sweet news By playing it to me with so sour a face.
speaker: JULIET

text: I am aweary, give me leave awhile. Fie, how my bones ache! What a jaunce have I had!
speaker: NURSE

text: I would thou hadst my bones, and I thy news. Nay, come, I pray thee speak. Good, good nurse, speak.
speaker: JULIET

text: Jesu, what haste! Can you not stay awhile? Do you not see that I am out of breath?
speaker: NURSE

text: How art thou out of breath when thou hast breath To say to me that thou art out of breath? The excuse that thou dost make in this delay Is longer than the tale thou dost excuse. Is thy news good or bad? Answer to that. Say either, and I'll stay the circumstance. Let me be satisfied, is't good or bad?
speaker: JULIET

text: Well, you have made a simple choice; you know not how to choose a man. Romeo? No, not he. Though his face be better than any man's, yet his leg excels all men's; and for a hand and a foot, and a body, though they be not to be talk'd on, yet they are past compare. He is not the flower of courtesy, but, I'll warrant him, as gentle as a lamb. Go thy ways, wench; serve God. What, have you din'd at home?
speaker: NURSE

text: No, no. But all this did I know before. What says he of our marriage? What of that?
speaker: JULIET

text: Lord, how my head aches! What a head have I! It beats as it would fall in twenty pieces. My back o' t' other side,- ah, my back, my back! Beshrew your heart for sending me about To catch my death with jauncing up and down!
speaker: NURSE

text: I' faith, I am sorry that thou art not well. Sweet, sweet, sweet nurse, tell me, what says my love?
speaker: JULIET

text: Your love says, like an honest gentleman, and a courteous, and a kind, and a handsome; and, I warrant, a virtuous- Where is your mother?
speaker: NURSE

text: Where is my mother? Why, she is within. Where should she be? How oddly thou repliest! 'Your love says, like an honest gentleman, "Where is your mother?"'
speaker: JULIET

text: O God's Lady dear! Are you so hot? Marry come up, I trow. Is this the poultice for my aching bones? Henceforward do your messages yourself.
speaker: NURSE

text: Here's such a coil! Come, what says Romeo?
speaker: JULIET

text: Have you got leave to go to shrift to-day?
speaker: NURSE

text: I have.
speaker: JULIET

text: Then hie you hence to Friar Laurence' cell; There stays a husband to make you a wife. Now comes the wanton blood up in your cheeks: They'll be in scarlet straight at any news. Hie you to church; I must another way, To fetch a ladder, by the which your love Must climb a bird's nest soon when it is dark. I am the drudge, and toil in your delight; But you shall bear the burthen soon at night. Go; I'll to dinner; hie you to the cell.
speaker: NURSE

text: Hie to high fortune! Honest nurse, farewell.
speaker: JULIET

text: Exeunt.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter Friar [Laurence] and Romeo.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: So smile the heavens upon this holy act That after-hours with sorrow chide us not!
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: Amen, amen! But come what sorrow can, It cannot countervail the exchange of joy That one short minute gives me in her sight. Do thou but close our hands with holy words, Then love-devouring death do what he dare- It is enough I may but call her mine.
speaker: ROMEO

text: These violent delights have violent ends And in their triumph die, like fire and powder, Which, as they kiss, consume. The sweetest honey Is loathsome in his own deliciousness And in the taste confounds the appetite. Therefore love moderately: long love doth so; Too swift arrives as tardy as too slow.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: Enter Juliet.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Here comes the lady. O, so light a foot
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Will ne'er wear out the everlasting flint.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: A lover may bestride the gossamer
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: That idles in the wanton summer air,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: And yet not fall; so light is vanity.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Good even to my ghostly confessor.
speaker: JULIET

text: Romeo shall thank thee, daughter, for us both.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: As much to him, else is his thanks too much.
speaker: JULIET

text: Ah, Juliet, if the measure of thy joy Be heap'd like mine, and that thy skill be more To blazon it, then sweeten with thy breath This neighbour air, and let rich music's tongue Unfold the imagin'd happiness that both Receive in either by this dear encounter.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Conceit, more rich in matter than in words, Brags of his substance, not of ornament. They are but beggars that can count their worth; But my true love is grown to such excess cannot sum up sum of half my wealth.
speaker: JULIET

text: Come, come with me, and we will make short work; For, by your leaves, you shall not stay alone Till Holy Church incorporate two in one.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: Exeunt.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
