text: A street.
speaker: MANTUA

text: Enter Romeo.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: If I may trust the flattering truth of sleep My dreams presage some joyful news at hand. My bosom's lord sits lightly in his throne, And all this day an unaccustom'd spirit Lifts me above the ground with cheerful thoughts. I dreamt my lady came and found me dead (Strange dream that gives a dead man leave to think!) And breath'd such life with kisses in my lips That I reviv'd and was an emperor. Ah me! how sweet is love itself possess'd, When but love's shadows are so rich in joy!
speaker: ROMEO

text: Enter Romeo's Man Balthasar, booted.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: News from Verona! How now, Balthasar?
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Dost thou not bring me letters from the friar?
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: How doth my lady? Is my father well?
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: How fares my Juliet? That I ask again,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: For nothing can be ill if she be well.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Then she is well, and nothing can be ill. Her body sleeps in Capel's monument, And her immortal part with angels lives. I saw her laid low in her kindred's vault And presently took post to tell it you. O, pardon me for bringing these ill news, Since you did leave it for my office, sir.
speaker: MAN

text: Is it e'en so? Then I defy you, stars! Thou knowest my lodging. Get me ink and paper And hire posthorses. I will hence to-night.
speaker: ROMEO

text: I do beseech you, sir, have patience. Your looks are pale and wild and do import Some misadventure.
speaker: MAN

text: Tush, thou art deceiv'd. Leave me and do the thing I bid thee do. Hast thou no letters to me from the friar?
speaker: ROMEO

text: No, my good lord.
speaker: MAN

text: No matter. Get thee gone And hire those horses. I'll be with thee straight.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Exit [Balthasar].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Well, Juliet, I will lie with thee to-night.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Let's see for means. O mischief, thou art swift
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: To enter in the thoughts of desperate men!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: I do remember an apothecary,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: And hereabouts 'a dwells, which late I noted
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: In tatt'red weeds, with overwhelming brows,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Culling of simples. Meagre were his looks,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Sharp misery had worn him to the bones;
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: And in his needy shop a tortoise hung,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: An alligator stuff'd, and other skins
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Of ill-shaped fishes; and about his shelves
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: A beggarly account of empty boxes,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Green earthen pots, bladders, and musty seeds,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Remnants of packthread, and old cakes of roses
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Were thinly scattered, to make up a show.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Noting this penury, to myself I said,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: 'An if a man did need a poison now
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Whose sale is present death in Mantua,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Here lives a caitiff wretch would sell it him.'
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: O, this same thought did but forerun my need,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: And this same needy man must sell it me.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: As I remember, this should be the house.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Being holiday, the beggar's shop is shut. What, ho!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: apothecary!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter Apothecary.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Who calls so loud?
speaker: APOTHECARY

text: Come hither, man. I see that thou art poor. Hold, there is forty ducats. Let me have A dram of poison, such soon-speeding gear As will disperse itself through all the veins That the life-weary taker mall fall dead, And that the trunk may be discharg'd of breath As violently as hasty powder fir'd Doth hurry from the fatal cannon's womb.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Such mortal drugs I have; but Mantua's law Is death to any he that utters them.
speaker: APOTHECARY

text: Art thou so bare and full of wretchedness And fearest to die? Famine is in thy cheeks, Need and oppression starveth in thine eyes, Contempt and beggary hangs upon thy back: The world is not thy friend, nor the world's law; The world affords no law to make thee rich; Then be not poor, but break it and take this.
speaker: ROMEO

text: My poverty but not my will consents.
speaker: APOTHECARY

text: I pay thy poverty and not thy will.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Put this in any liquid thing you will And drink it off, and if you had the strength Of twenty men, it would dispatch you straight.
speaker: APOTHECARY

text: There is thy gold- worse poison to men's souls, Doing more murther in this loathsome world, Than these poor compounds that thou mayst not sell. I sell thee poison; thou hast sold me none.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Buy food and get thyself in flesh. Come, cordial and not poison, go with me To Juliet's grave; for there must I use thee.
speaker: FAREWELL

text: Exeunt.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter Friar John to Friar Laurence.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Holy Franciscan friar, brother, ho!
speaker: JOHN

text: Enter Friar Laurence.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: This same should be the voice of Friar John. Welcome from Mantua. What says Romeo? Or, if his mind be writ, give me his letter.
speaker: LAUR

text: Going to find a barefoot brother out, One of our order, to associate me Here in this city visiting the sick, And finding him, the searchers of the town, Suspecting that we both were in a house Where the infectious pestilence did reign, Seal'd up the doors, and would not let us forth, So that my speed to Mantua there was stay'd.
speaker: JOHN

text: Who bare my letter, then, to Romeo?
speaker: LAUR

text: I could not send it- here it is again- Nor get a messenger to bring it thee, So fearful were they of infection.
speaker: JOHN

text: Unhappy fortune! By my brotherhood, The letter was not nice, but full of charge, Of dear import; and the neglecting it May do much danger. Friar John, go hence, Get me an iron crow and bring it straight Unto my cell.
speaker: LAUR

text: Brother, I'll go and bring it thee.                 Exit.
speaker: JOHN

text: Now, must I to the monument alone. Within this three hours will fair Juliet wake. She will beshrew me much that Romeo Hath had no notice of these accidents; But I will write again to Mantua, And keep her at my cell till Romeo come- Poor living corse, clos'd in a dead man's tomb!        Exit.
speaker: LAUR

text: Enter Paris and his Page with flowers and [a torch].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Give me thy torch, boy. Hence, and stand aloof. Yet put it out, for I would not be seen. Under yond yew tree lay thee all along, Holding thine ear close to the hollow ground. So shall no foot upon the churchyard tread (Being loose, unfirm, with digging up of graves) But thou shalt hear it. Whistle then to me, As signal that thou hear'st something approach. Give me those flowers. Do as I bid thee, go.
speaker: PAR

text: [aside] I am almost afraid to stand alone Here in the churchyard; yet I will adventure.     [Retires.]
speaker: PAGE

text: Sweet flower, with flowers thy bridal bed I strew (O woe! thy canopy is dust and stones) Which with sweet water nightly I will dew; Or, wanting that, with tears distill'd by moans. The obsequies that I for thee will keep Nightly shall be to strew thy grave and weep. Whistle Boy. The boy gives warning something doth approach. What cursed foot wanders this way to-night To cross my obsequies and true love's rite? What, with a torch? Muffle me, night, awhile.     [Retires.]
speaker: PAR

text: Enter Romeo, and Balthasar with a torch, a mattock,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: and a crow of iron.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Give me that mattock and the wrenching iron. Hold, take this letter. Early in the morning See thou deliver it to my lord and father. Give me the light. Upon thy life I charge thee, Whate'er thou hearest or seest, stand all aloof And do not interrupt me in my course. Why I descend into this bed of death Is partly to behold my lady's face, But chiefly to take thence from her dead finger A precious ring- a ring that I must use In dear employment. Therefore hence, be gone. But if thou, jealous, dost return to pry In what I farther shall intend to do, By heaven, I will tear thee joint by joint And strew this hungry churchyard with thy limbs. The time and my intents are savage-wild, More fierce and more inexorable far Than empty tigers or the roaring sea.
speaker: ROMEO

text: I will be gone, sir, and not trouble you.
speaker: BAL

text: So shalt thou show me friendship. Take thou that. Live, and be prosperous; and farewell, good fellow.
speaker: ROMEO

text: [aside] For all this same, I'll hide me hereabout. His looks I fear, and his intents I doubt.        [Retires.]
speaker: BAL

text: Thou detestable maw, thou womb of death, Gorg'd with the dearest morsel of the earth, Thus I enforce thy rotten jaws to open, And in despite I'll cram thee with more food. Romeo opens the tomb.
speaker: ROMEO

text: This is that banish'd haughty Montague That murd'red my love's cousin- with which grief It is supposed the fair creature died- And here is come to do some villanous shame To the dead bodies. I will apprehend him. Stop thy unhallowed toil, vile Montague! Can vengeance be pursu'd further than death? Condemned villain, I do apprehend thee. Obey, and go with me; for thou must die.
speaker: PAR

text: I must indeed; and therefore came I hither. Good gentle youth, tempt not a desp'rate man. Fly hence and leave me. Think upon these gone; Let them affright thee. I beseech thee, youth, Put not another sin upon my head By urging me to fury. O, be gone! By heaven, I love thee better than myself, For I come hither arm'd against myself. Stay not, be gone. Live, and hereafter say A madman's mercy bid thee run away.
speaker: ROMEO

text: I do defy thy conjuration And apprehend thee for a felon here.
speaker: PAR

text: Wilt thou provoke me? Then have at thee, boy! They fight.
speaker: ROMEO

text: O Lord, they fight! I will go call the watch.
speaker: PAGE

text: Exit. Paris falls.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: O, I am slain! If thou be merciful, Open the tomb, lay me with Juliet.                   [Dies.]
speaker: PAR

text: In faith, I will. Let me peruse this face. Mercutio's kinsman, noble County Paris! What said my man when my betossed soul Did not attend him as we rode? I think He told me Paris should have married Juliet. Said he not so? or did I dream it so? Or am I mad, hearing him talk of Juliet To think it was so? O, give me thy hand, One writ with me in sour misfortune's book! I'll bury thee in a triumphant grave. A grave? O, no, a lanthorn, slaught'red youth, For here lies Juliet, and her beauty makes This vault a feasting presence full of light. Death, lie thou there, by a dead man interr'd.
speaker: ROMEO

text: Lays him in the tomb.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: How oft when men are at the point of death
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Have they been merry! which their keepers call
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: A lightning before death. O, how may I
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Call this a lightning? O my love! my wife!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Death, that hath suck'd the honey of thy breath,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Hath had no power yet upon thy beauty.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Thou art not conquer'd. Beauty's ensign yet
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Is crimson in thy lips and in thy cheeks,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: And death's pale flag is not advanced there.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Tybalt, liest thou there in thy bloody sheet?
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: O, what more favour can I do to thee
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Than with that hand that cut thy youth in twain
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: To sunder his that was thine enemy?
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Forgive me, cousin. Ah, dear Juliet,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Why art thou yet so fair? Shall I believe
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: That unsubstantial Death is amorous,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: And that the lean abhorred monster keeps
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Thee here in dark to be his paramour?
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: For fear of that I still will stay with thee
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: And never from this palace of dim night
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Depart again. Here, here will I remain
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: With worms that are thy chambermaids. O, here
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Will I set up my everlasting rest
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: And shake the yoke of inauspicious stars
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: From this world-wearied flesh. Eyes, look your last!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Arms, take your last embrace! and, lips, O you
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: The doors of breath, seal with a righteous kiss
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: A dateless bargain to engrossing death!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Come, bitter conduct; come, unsavoury guide!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Thou desperate pilot, now at once run on
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: The dashing rocks thy seasick weary bark!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Here's to my love! [Drinks.] O true apothecary!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Thy drugs are quick. Thus with a kiss I die.          Falls.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter Friar [Laurence], with lanthorn, crow, and spade.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Saint Francis be my speed! how oft to-night Have my old feet stumbled at graves! Who's there?
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: Here's one, a friend, and one that knows you well.
speaker: BAL

text: Bliss be upon you! Tell me, good my friend, What torch is yond that vainly lends his light To grubs and eyeless skulls? As I discern, It burneth in the Capels' monument.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: It doth so, holy sir; and there's my master, One that you love.
speaker: BAL

text: Who is it?
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: Romeo.
speaker: BAL

text: How long hath he been there?
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: Full half an hour.
speaker: BAL

text: Go with me to the vault.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: I dare not, sir. My master knows not but I am gone hence, And fearfully did menace me with death If I did stay to look on his intents.
speaker: BAL

text: Stay then; I'll go alone. Fear comes upon me. O, much I fear some ill unthrifty thing.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: As I did sleep under this yew tree here, I dreamt my master and another fought, And that my master slew him.
speaker: BAL

text: Romeo! Alack, alack, what blood is this which stains The stony entrance of this sepulchre? What mean these masterless and gory swords To lie discolour'd by this place of peace? [Enters the tomb.] Romeo! O, pale! Who else? What, Paris too? And steep'd in blood? Ah, what an unkind hour Is guilty of this lamentable chance! The lady stirs. Juliet rises.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: O comfortable friar! where is my lord? I do remember well where I should be, And there I am. Where is my Romeo?
speaker: JULIET

text: I hear some noise. Lady, come from that nest Of death, contagion, and unnatural sleep. A greater power than we can contradict Hath thwarted our intents. Come, come away. Thy husband in thy bosom there lies dead; And Paris too. Come, I'll dispose of thee Among a sisterhood of holy nuns. Stay not to question, for the watch is coming. Come, go, good Juliet. I dare no longer stay.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: Go, get thee hence, for I will not away.
speaker: JULIET

text: Exit [Friar].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: What's here? A cup, clos'd in my true love's hand?
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Poison, I see, hath been his timeless end.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: O churl! drunk all, and left no friendly drop
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: To help me after? I will kiss thy lips.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Haply some poison yet doth hang on them
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: To make me die with a restorative.             [Kisses him.]
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Thy lips are warm!
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: [within] Lead, boy. Which way? Yea, noise? Then I'll be brief. O happy dagger!
speaker: CHIEF WATCH

text: Snatches Romeo's dagger.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: This is thy sheath; there rest, and let me die.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: She stabs herself and falls [on Romeo's body].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter [Paris's] Boy and Watch.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: This is the place. There, where the torch doth burn.
speaker: BOY

text: 'the ground is bloody. Search about the churchyard. Go, some of you; whoe'er you find attach.
speaker: CHIEF WATCH

text: Exeunt some of the Watch.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Pitiful sight! here lies the County slain;
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: And Juliet bleeding, warm, and newly dead,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Who here hath lain this two days buried.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Go, tell the Prince; run to the Capulets;
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Raise up the Montagues; some others search.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Exeunt others of the Watch.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: We see the ground whereon these woes do lie,
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: But the true ground of all these piteous woes
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: We cannot without circumstance descry.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Enter [some of the Watch,] with Romeo's Man [Balthasar].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: 2. Watch. Here's Romeo's man. We found him in the churchyard.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Hold him in safety till the Prince come hither.
speaker: CHIEF WATCH

text: Enter Friar [Laurence] and another Watchman.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: 3. Watch. Here is a friar that trembles, sighs, and weeps.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: We took this mattock and this spade from him
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: As he was coming from this churchyard side.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: A great suspicion! Stay the friar too.
speaker: CHIEF WATCH

text: Enter the Prince [and Attendants].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: What misadventure is so early up, That calls our person from our morning rest?
speaker: PRINCE

text: Enter Capulet and his Wife [with others].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: What should it be, that they so shriek abroad?
speaker: CAPULET

text: The people in the street cry 'Romeo,' Some 'Juliet,' and some 'Paris'; and all run, With open outcry, toward our monument.
speaker: WIFE

text: What fear is this which startles in our ears?
speaker: PRINCE

text: Sovereign, here lies the County Paris slain; And Romeo dead; and Juliet, dead before, Warm and new kill'd.
speaker: CHIEF WATCH

text: Search, seek, and know how this foul murder comes.
speaker: PRINCE

text: Here is a friar, and slaughter'd Romeo's man, With instruments upon them fit to open These dead men's tombs.
speaker: CHIEF WATCH

text: O heavens! O wife, look how our daughter bleeds! This dagger hath mista'en, for, lo, his house Is empty on the back of Montague, And it missheathed in my daughter's bosom!
speaker: CAPULET

text: O me! this sight of death is as a bell That warns my old age to a sepulchre.
speaker: WIFE

text: Enter Montague [and others].
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION

text: Come, Montague; for thou art early up To see thy son and heir more early down.
speaker: PRINCE

text: Alas, my liege, my wife is dead to-night! Grief of my son's exile hath stopp'd her breath. What further woe conspires against mine age?
speaker: MONTAGUE

text: Look, and thou shalt see.
speaker: PRINCE

text: O thou untaught! what manners is in this, To press before thy father to a grave?
speaker: MONTAGUE

text: Seal up the mouth of outrage for a while, Till we can clear these ambiguities And know their spring, their head, their true descent; And then will I be general of your woes And lead you even to death. Meantime forbear, And let mischance be slave to patience. Bring forth the parties of suspicion.
speaker: PRINCE

text: I am the greatest, able to do least, Yet most suspected, as the time and place Doth make against me, of this direful murther; And here I stand, both to impeach and purge Myself condemned and myself excus'd.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: Then say at once what thou dost know in this.
speaker: PRINCE

text: I will be brief, for my short date of breath Is not so long as is a tedious tale. Romeo, there dead, was husband to that Juliet; And she, there dead, that Romeo's faithful wife. I married them; and their stol'n marriage day Was Tybalt's doomsday, whose untimely death Banish'd the new-made bridegroom from this city; For whom, and not for Tybalt, Juliet pin'd. You, to remove that siege of grief from her, Betroth'd and would have married her perforce To County Paris. Then comes she to me And with wild looks bid me devise some mean To rid her from this second marriage, Or in my cell there would she kill herself. Then gave I her (so tutored by my art) A sleeping potion; which so took effect As I intended, for it wrought on her The form of death. Meantime I writ to Romeo That he should hither come as this dire night To help to take her from her borrowed grave, Being the time the potion's force should cease. But he which bore my letter, Friar John, Was stay'd by accident, and yesternight Return'd my letter back. Then all alone At the prefixed hour of her waking Came I to take her from her kindred's vault; Meaning to keep her closely at my cell Till I conveniently could send to Romeo. But when I came, some minute ere the time Of her awaking, here untimely lay The noble Paris and true Romeo dead. She wakes; and I entreated her come forth And bear this work of heaven with patience; But then a noise did scare me from the tomb, And she, too desperate, would not go with me, But, as it seems, did violence on herself. All this I know, and to the marriage Her nurse is privy; and if aught in this Miscarried by my fault, let my old life Be sacrific'd, some hour before his time, Unto the rigour of severest law.
speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE

text: We still have known thee for a holy man. Where's Romeo's man? What can he say in this?
speaker: PRINCE

text: I brought my master news of Juliet's death; And then in post he came from Mantua To this same place, to this same monument. This letter he early bid me give his father, And threat'ned me with death, going in the vault, If I departed not and left him there.
speaker: BAL

text: Give me the letter. I will look on it. Where is the County's page that rais'd the watch? Sirrah, what made your master in this place?
speaker: PRINCE

text: He came with flowers to strew his lady's grave; And bid me stand aloof, and so I did. Anon comes one with light to ope the tomb; And by-and-by my master drew on him; And then I ran away to call the watch.
speaker: BOY

text: This letter doth make good the friar's words, Their course of love, the tidings of her death; And here he writes that he did buy a poison Of a poor pothecary, and therewithal Came to this vault to die, and lie with Juliet. Where be these enemies? Capulet, Montague, See what a scourge is laid upon your hate, That heaven finds means to kill your joys with love! And I, for winking at you, discords too, Have lost a brace of kinsmen. All are punish'd.
speaker: PRINCE

text: O brother Montague, give me thy hand. This is my daughter's jointure, for no more Can I demand.
speaker: CAPULET

text: But I can give thee more; For I will raise her statue in pure gold, That whiles Verona by that name is known, There shall no figure at such rate be set As that of true and faithful Juliet.
speaker: MONTAGUE

text: As rich shall Romeo's by his lady's lie- Poor sacrifices of our enmity!
speaker: CAPULET

text: A glooming peace this morning with it brings. The sun for sorrow will not show his head. Go hence, to have more talk of these sad things; Some shall be pardon'd, and some punished; For never was a story of more woe Than this of Juliet and her Romeo.
speaker: PRINCE

text: Exeunt omnes.
speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
